project_description.text: The USDA Forest Service proposes regulations and administrative instructions under Executive Order 11644 to control off-road vehicle (ORV) use on the 187 million acres of National Forest System lands, to be added to 36 CFR Part 295 and the Forest Service Manual. The action would designate specific areas and trails where ORV use is allowed, restricted, or prohibited, with designations to be completed by December 31, 1976, in order to protect resources, promote user safety, and reduce conflicts among users.

affected_community.text: Affected groups include ORV users such as four-wheel drivers, motorcyclists, snowmobilers, and dune buggy and all-terrain vehicle operators, as well as hikers, horseback riders, hunters, anglers, and others seeking quiet recreation. Rural and resort communities that depend on ORV sales and tourism are also affected, along with mining claimants, oil and gas and utility operators, Indian tribes on adjoining lands, ORV manufacturers, and federal, state, and local agencies that need access to forest lands.

alternatives_overview.text: Two main alternatives were considered. Alternative A would immediately prohibit all ORV use on National Forest lands and then study where use should later be allowed, while Alternative B would continue to allow ORV use and study which areas and trails need controls. Alternative B was selected because it builds on existing programs, meets the intent of the Executive Order, and causes less immediate disruption; both alternatives were estimated to cost about 34,000 staff-days and $3.5 million to set up, plus about $6 million per year to run.

environmental_impact.text: Controlling ORV use is expected to reduce harm to air and water quality, soils, vegetation, wildlife and habitat, tree regeneration, and noise levels, while improving safety and reducing conflicts among forest users. Unavoidable negative effects include visual impacts from signs, possible drops in needed game harvests where hunter access is limited, and continued damage from vehicles exempted from the rules, especially those used for prospecting and mineral development under the General Mining Act of 1872.

public_response.text: Of 566 written responses, most supported Alternative B, though conservation groups such as the Sierra Club and Izaak Walton League generally favored immediate prohibition and called the Draft EIS too general and superficial on impacts. About 75 percent of comments on the mining exemption opposed it as unenforceable and inconsistent with Executive Order 11644, and many respondents urged posting open rather than closed areas, a faster implementation schedule, longer public comment periods, and stronger enforcement. EPA, the Department of the Interior, and other federal agencies recommended better monitoring, interagency coordination, and protection of cultural resources and adjacent parks.

value.name: No Action
value.description: Continue existing ad hoc management of off-road vehicle use on National Forest System lands without implementing new unified regulations or administrative instructions under Executive Order 11644.

value.name: Proposed Action (Regulated ORV Use)
value.description: Establish policies and procedures through regulations and administrative instructions to control and direct off-road vehicle use on National Forest System lands, protecting resources, promoting user safety, and minimizing conflicts among various uses.

themes.justification: The EIS centers on regulating off-road vehicle use across 187 million acres of National Forest lands to protect soils, vegetation, wildlife habitat, and water quality (Habitat Conservation and Biodiversity; Infrastructure Impacts on Landscapes), while the core action involves USDA Forest Service rulemaking, designation of ORV areas, and interagency coordination under Executive Order 11644 (Environmental Regulation and Policy; Institutional Power and Resource Management). The designation of specific trails and areas for ORV mobility also directly engages Transportation Infrastructure themes.

location.note: Nationwide policy EIS covering all 187 million acres of National Forest System lands regarding off-road vehicle regulations; no single geographic site.
